I've had similar issue.
In my case problem was in recursive introspection of proxy attribute when I tried to access it.
https://www.pykka.org/en/latest/#actor-proxies
https://www.pykka.org/en/latest/#traversable-attributes-on-proxies

Instead:
```
# proxy as attr
self.proxy = self.actor_ref.proxy()

#later in code
self.proxy.attr_or_method() #there starts recursive introspection
```
try to use this:
```
#method that return proxy
def proxy(self):
    return self.actor_ref.proxy()

#later in code
self.proxy().attr_or_method() #no recursice introspection
```

Yeah, exactly. And this means that SO_REUSEPORT also has security
implications, because it allows for a limited form of "port hijacking".

Note that IIRC SO_REUSEPORT on *BSD and MacOS means something different
though.

If you *do* want to use SO_REUSEPORT you need both the AttributeError check
and the OSError check -- socket.SO_REUSEPORT is defined iff Python was
*built* on a system that had SO_REUSEPORT support, and the setsockopt call
succeeds if *running* on a system that has SO_REUSEPORT support.

Windows will return immediately (and an error) when trying to select() on three empty sets, this differs from the POSIX api which will block if given a timeout. A work-around might look like:

`import curio
import selectors
import socket

dummy_socket = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)

async def countdown(n):
	while n > 0:
		print('T-minus', n)
		await curio.sleep(1)
		n -= 1

if __name__ == '__main__':
	selector = selectors.DefaultSelector()
	selector.register(dummy_socket, selectors.EVENT_READ)
	curio.run(countdown(100000), selector=selector)
`

Based on your error messages, it looks like you're missing the Python
header files needed to build C extension modules. As you noted, the C
version is not required though. The easiest way to get up and running is
probably to just clone the git repository and comment out the extension
module in the setup.py file. It'll then install the pure Python and
ctypes-based implementation. The ctypes version is almost as fast as the C
extension so it'll probably be good enough.

Yes, I've also been thinking how to add support for linters. It has been on my wishlist for a long time.  Thanks for bringing it up

1. One problem arises around which files to lint. Each tool can have tool-specific files. A way to solve that could be to instead add a --lint flag to the backend, such as there already are for --setup or --build-only

2. Yes. They are sharing more and more code, and some tools are actually a bit hard to categorize. I would need a good reason to make this change however. Do you have  any example?

There was a patch (https://github.com/olofk/fusesoc/pull/140) some time ago to do something similar. My reason at the time not to accept it was that I thought this could just as well be handled by a shell script. That would also make it easier to have conditions, such as only run synthesis if lint succeeds. I think you can already call most of the steps in the build process separately, even if it's not very clear how. --setup only runs the configure stage, --build-only runs configure+build, --keep will only run the simulation without rebuilding. I guess this could need some cleanup :)

Hi Paul,
Good that we are having this conversation, because I was only focusing on my own use case, which is pretty straightforward: the two files have the same number of columns and rows, so there is anything to "match" only tell if each "cell" is within the tolerance or now. Certainly for a general purpose tool such as daff I can't make that assumption. So before we start talking algorithms, let's try to focus on the use cases. 

Let's start from the threshold set to zero. In that case, equality is well behaved and we can treat that as you currently do for whitespace and case sensitivity. 

But of course there's more. For the non-zero threshold, my use case is certainly one, which of course is important to me (otherwise I wouldn't be here), but I don't want to make the floating point option restrict to that use case.

Another use case could be comparing, say a table of the distance of the planets from the sun from two different wikipedia pages, that would look like the following

```
Planet name                 distance (km)
Mercury                     57,909,175
Venus                       108,208,930
Earth                       149,597,890
Mars                        227,936,640
Jupiter                     778,412,010
Saturn                      1,426,725,400
Uranus                      2,870,972,200
Neptune                     4,498,252,900
```
but maybe differ in precision and may have different lines (probably a better example would have been a table of densities, which one table reporting some materials and another an overlapping list of materials, but you get the idea).

So I do we want to proceed in this case? In my opinion there are two separate step (which at the moment are unified, if my understanding is correct), namely "matching" lines, and then "diffing" them. For example, if two lines both have "Venus" as planet name, they "match". The value they report may or may not "diff" and that may depend on the threshold being used.

So, how about for the matching part of algorithm we use the one you are currently using, simply ignoring the floating point values that are not equal in the two tables? This should be able to deal with both my use case (which may or may not have text, but has certainly some number that are identical and would "match") and the general case like the one above.

> But, is the encoding performed only when using str() on the value?

Natural representation for `OctetString` is `bytes`. That's because ASN.1 `OctetString` is never meant to be a human-readable text, but a vague binary data. However, if you try to render  `OctetString` value in a text context (e.g. do `str()` on it), it will `.encode()` its contents into human-readable text using the encoding associated with this `OctetString` instance.

Likewise, if you initialize `OctetString` with a `str` type, the value will be automatically `decode()`-ed into bytes using the encoding associated with this `OctetString` instance.

Default encoding is iso-8859-1 (because it is most neutral to the contents), however you could set different encoding on `OctetString` [instantiation](http://pyasn1.sourceforge.net/docs/type/univ/octetstring.html#pyasn1.type.univ.OctetString).

> But what is sent over the wire?

Always bytes. Encoding is used in only two cases: initializing `OctetString` from of turning its payload  into text.

Note that this logic is reversed for ASN.1 [character types](http://pyasn1.sourceforge.net/docs/type/char/utf8string.html#asn-1-type) as they are designed to represent text, not binary data.

Does it make sense? ;-)

Thanks for the report. Yes, it's true that Popen doesn't work as expected with PATHEXT. A [search of PATHEXT](http://bugs.python.org/issue?%40columns=id%2Cactivity%2Ctitle%2Ccreator%2Cassignee%2Cstatus%2Ctype&%40sort=-activity&%40filter=status&%40action=searchid&ignore=file%3Acontent&%40search_text=PATHEXT&submit=search&status=-1%2C1%2C2%2C3) on bugs.python.org yields quite a few related issues.

As I understand it (though I have no reference), Microsoft has commented on this issue and has indicated that a fix to generally support PATHEXT in the CreateProcess API (which Popen uses) is unlikely to happen, and that wrapper executables should be used for maximum compatibility.

I believe there is room for the Python code to work around this limitation, and have Popen resolve the PATHEXT to the hosting executable and run the program that way, but that would be outside the scope of hgtools.

Another option might be to invoke Popen with shell=True, which will use a shell process such as cmd.exe to resolve the PATHEXT script. I'm reluctant to simply make this change as I'm unaware what other implications that might have.

As this project is largely defunct in deference to [setuptools_scm](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/setuptools_scm), I suggest the issue be taken up there. If the issue is fixed there, then I'd be happy to accept the same behavior for this library.

In the meantime, I'm marking this as wontfix, as I won't have time to invest in troubleshooting or testing, but I welcome pull requests.

me: In contrast, the alert @a 3d with @s 10/15 10pm would trigger the alert once

you: The sentence beginning with "In contrast" confused me as I think the alert 
would trigger on 10/18 at 10 pm rather than on 10/12.

Your confusion confuses me.  The alert will trigger 3 days before the starting time - not 3 days after. Right?

The intent of the error message 

	the value of @b should be an integer: '@b 3d’

is to convey the information that an integer is required and to show the entry actually given. It could, I suppose, be modified 

	the value of @b should be an integer: '@b 3d’ is invalid


If instead the error message was

	the value of @b should be an integer: '@b 3’ would be valid

then the author would have to look back at the original entry, possibly dragging the error message box out of the way to do so, to see what was actually entered. It’s not obvious to me that this is an improvement. Assuming that the author knows what an integer is, displaying a 3 as an example of an integer doesn’t add all that much unless the intent is something like “you entered “3d” but I’m guessing that you meant “3”.

It might seem easy to process a string such as “3d” and guess that the intent of the author was the integer 3 but what about “3w”? Should this too be converted to 3 or should it be interpreted as 3 weeks and be converted to 21 or as a possible typo where the “w” was supposed to be “2” and be converted to 32?  Isn’t it enough to say that an integer is required, that “3d”, “3w” or whatever is (thus) not a valid entry and leave it to the author to make the correction?

There are many situations in etm where an integer is required, e.g., @b, @p (priority), &t (total number of repetitions in @r entries), &q (queue position in @j entries) and so forth. Whenever any of these is processed, if the entry is not an integer, the same “an integer is required but here is what you provided” message is generated. Can you see how difficult it would be to make good integer guesses for “@b Oct 12”, “@p low” or “&t 5d”? 

-Dan

Some other API ideas in this regard: something like `TorState.on_circuit_built(some_callback)`, or `.on_circuit('built', cb)`). And then `some_callback` would require the same signature as the current `CircuitEventListener.on_circuit_built`.

I've also seen APIs kind-of like this designed around a coroutine, mostly in asycio libraries (e.g. see the WebSockets API in aiohttp). Looks good in examples, but I'm not sure how it works in practice. The equivalent here would be something along these lines (using `@inlineCallback` instead of async/await):

```
@inlineCallbacks
def circuit_listener(state):
    while True:
        event = yield state.next_circuit_event()
        # deal with event, according to details in "event"
```

It's a bit nicer using async/await, something like:

```
async def circuit_listener(state):
    async for event in state.circuit_events():
        # deal with event, according to details in "event"
```

But actually the granularity you'd (very likely) want above might be per-circuit (e.g. `async for event in circuit.events()`).

```
color[0], color[9]. color[10]. color[11], color[12], color[13], color[14], color[15], color[8], color[9]. color[10]. color[11], color[12], color[13], color[14], color[15]

- pysam now depends on sysconfig and it's not available in Python 2.6
- pysam does not build on 3.2 and 3.3 due to something related to https://bugs.python.org/issue16754. Automatically excluding binary files from analysis in theory makes sense. However, detecting binaries is non trivial. The most common approach seems to be checking for 0 bytes as used by [Subversion](http://subversion.apache.org/faq.html#binary-files) and [gitattributes](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitattributes). The actual [code fom git](http://git.kernel.org/?p=git/git.git;a=blob;f=xdiff-interface.c;h=0e2c169227ad29b5bf546c6c1b97e1a1d8ed7409;hb=HEAD) is:

```c
#define FIRST_FEW_BYTES 8000
 int buffer_is_binary(const char *ptr, unsigned long size)
 {
         if (FIRST_FEW_BYTES < size)
                 size = FIRST_FEW_BYTES;
         return !!memchr(ptr, 0, size);
 }
```
In case of pygments it would make sense to treat files with headers for UTF-16 and UTF-32 as text despite plenty of 0 bytes in it.

I've narrowed this down to be a variation of #280 

When the SQL statement contains more than one statement (i.e. more complex transactions, try catch blocks, etc...)  and fast_executemany=True,  the SQLDescribeParam call doesn't work and thus VARCHARs are allocated a default buffer size of 510 bytes. 

The following code will reproduce the error (notte the "print" statement in the SQL, but this could be TRY/CATCH BLOCKS, etc...

```
import pyodbc
import sys

print('Python version ' + sys.version)
print('pyodbc version ' + pyodbc.version)

driver = 'ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server'
server = 'localhost'
database = 'db'
user = 'sa'
password = 'password'

conn_string = f'DRIVER={{{driver}}};' \
                   f'PORT=1433;SERVER={server};' \
                   f'DATABASE={database};' \
                   f'UID={user};' \
                   f'PWD={password};' \
                   f'ColumnEncryption=Enabled;' \
                   f'TrustServerCertificate=yes;'

cnxn = pyodbc.connect(conn_string, autocommit=True)

crsr = cnxn.cursor()

crsr.fast_executemany = True

crsr.execute('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS tmp')
crsr.execute('CREATE TABLE tmp (txt VARCHAR(1024))')

value_to_insert = 'a' * 256

params = [(value_to_insert,), (value_to_insert,)]

sql = """
print 'do nothing';
INSERT INTO tmp (txt) VALUES (?);
"""

crsr.setinputsizes((512,))
crsr.executemany(sql, params)

print(crsr.execute('SELECT txt from tmp').fetchall())
```

(note that in the actual application, the SQL is significantly more complicated; this is the simplest case that will reproduce the error) 

This produces the output with errors:
```
Python version 3.6.3 (default, Nov  6 2017, 10:55:10)
[GCC 6.3.0 20170516]
pyodbc version 4.0.23
pyodbc.ProgrammingError: ('String data, right truncation: length 512 buffer 510', 'HY000')

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

SystemError: <built-in function utf_16_le_encode> returned a result with an error set

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

SystemError: encoding with 'utf-16le' codec failed (SystemError: <built-in function utf_16_le_encode> returned a result with an error set)

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

SystemError: <built-in function utf_16_le_encode> returned a result with an error set

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "pypirls/memerrortest.py", line 40, in <module>
    crsr.executemany(sql, params)
SystemError: encoding with 'utf-16le' codec failed (SystemError: <built-in function utf_16_le_encode> returned a result with an error set)
```

However, changing any one of the below will avoid the exception:
* Removing the "print" statement from the SQL
* Setting the number of 'a' characters in the inserted varchar to 255 (fits in buffer size)
* Setting `fast_executemany` to `False` 

Note that unlike the solution in the [wiki](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Tips-and-Tricks-by-Database-Platform#using-fast_executemany-with-a-temporary-table), a temporary table is not being used, and `ColumnEncryption=Enabled`

It's unclear to me why this problem happens when `fast_executemany=True` but not when `False` as I would think that `SQLDescribeParameter` would have the same behaviour in either case, but I'm going to guess it's because in the latter case, a memory array doesn't need to be pre-allocated.

> Alternatively, what about raising an error instead? I know this is not standards-compliant, but if someone really needs that functionality they can just increment the value themselves and try again, I believe.

:+1: for raising an exception in this case.

The caller should probably know whether the subkey at a particular index is invalid and be responsible for skipping it. (By the way, I'm pretty sure this is exactly what the standard calls for.) Consider the case where index `x` is invalid if one *didn't* raise an exception:

```python
i = x - 1
subkey = node.subkey(i) # returns subkey for x -1
i = x
subkey = node.subkey(i) # silently returns subkey for x + 1
i = x + 1
subkey = node.subkey(i) # returns already-seen subkey
```

If one were to silently move onto the next valid key, then the caller has to perform at least one look-ahead to make sure a distinct subkey is returned for every single call to `subkey`. This is probably not what you want.

It's not actually `Cerebro` but the *data feeds* which have an open file descriptor which with Python `3.4` (and at least also `3.5`) cannot be pickled.

Python `2.7` doesn't exhibit the same.

The actual behavior is a side effect of an optimization added following a request in #135 to avoid re-loading the datas over and over again in subprocesses.

There is no actual need to pass the open file descriptor because it is never used again.

In the meanwhile you can do the following:

```python
cerebro.run(optdatas=False)
```

And data will not be preloaded before breaking down into processes

Exactly. This is why the contextvar that's to be used for caching needs to be visible – and if the runtime needs to set it anyway, then it could do so as soon as it starts up and creates its initial context: zero overhead and no need for adding support for a sentinel dance in the first place.

Option (2) seems reasonable as Guido formulated it, as I understand this will be accompanied by a way to make a factory like
```python
class C:
    bad_attr: List[int] = []  # Error
    attr: List[int] = field(factory=list)  # OK
```
and a clear error message like ``ValueError: using [] as a default value is unsafe. Use factory=list instead``.

With this change, the default would be that you can't order classes, but when you compare them they compare by value, not identity (as is the default for regular classes). I think that's a reasonable default for something focused on the fields within classes.

Who would see these annotations? Definitely not a static type checker like mypy, PyCharm or pytype.

I guess someone could introspect the annotations at runtime. But that also seems an unlikely use case (usually that's done for the benefit of argument checking, and I think you don't need that). So I think it's overkill and perhaps a little bit wasteful (all those functions would have `__annotations__` dicts that nobody will look at).

PS. The `self` / `_self` parameter should almost never have a type annotation (except for something like a `__copy__` method or a `create` factory class method); `other` should be annotated `object` or `Any` (EIBTI).

I added a blinker signal named `request_token_fetched` in https://github.com/lepture/flask-oauthlib/pull/205. If you want to fix this issue by patching you could do this:

``` python
from flask import session, request
from flask_oauthlib.contrib.client.signals import request_token_fetched
from oauthlib.common import add_params_to_uri

twitter = oauth.remote_app(
    name='twitter',
    version='1',
    endpoint_url='https://api.twitter.com/1.1/',
    request_token_url='https://api.twitter.com/oauth/request_token',
    access_token_url='https://api.twitter.com/oauth/access_token',
    authorization_url='https://api.twitter.com/oauth/authorize')

@request_token_fetched.connect_via(twitter)
def request_token_compliance_fixes(sender, response):
    session['request_token'] = response['oauth_token']

@app.route('/auth/twitter/callback')
def oauth_twitter_callback():
    # fixes nonstandard oauth1.0a
    if 'oauth_token' not in request.args and \
        'oauth_verifier' in request.args and \
        'request_token' in session:
        request.url = add_params_to_uri(request.url, [('oauth_token', session['request_token'])])

    response = twitter.authorized_response()
    if response:
        return repr({'access_token': response['oauth_token']})
    return 'denied'
```

The first issue is that you're not using the right encoding. The `unicodecsv` reader requires a binary-opened file. the `reader` function takes an `encoding` argument, which defaults to `utf-8`, which is incorrect for you.

Unfortunately, `unicodecsv` doesn't yet support `utf-16`, because the underlying reader doesn't allow for any null bytes. We have talked about a couple ideas for fixing it, but it hasn't been implemented yet.

For those of you still struggling with this issue, here's what I did. 
If you try to serialize a model in Flask that has a ChoiceType field, it will return an instance of choice type, not the actual choice. To access the choice, just use ChoiceType.code:

following on from the example above:

```python
class Card(Base):
    STATUS_VALUES = [
        ('Active', 'Active'),
        ('Inactive', 'Inactive'),
        ('Abandoned', 'Abandoned'),
        ('Installed', 'Installed'),
        ('Removed', 'Removed'),
        ('Other', 'Other'),
    ]
    status = db.Column(ChoiceType(STATUS_VALUES))
```
you'd create a getter to access the status like so:

```python
class Card(base):
    # your code here
    @property
    def status(self):
          return self.status.code
```

I think you've already determined that this in invalid CSV data. the `csv` module has to make an arbitrary choice about what to do with invalid data, and the choice here isn't what you wanted. This being a backport of the Python 3 library, my goal is that this library should behave the same as Python 3's version, which displays the same behavior that you're demonstrating here.

I suspect that your case is even more nuanced than what you've described here, but for the purpose of attempting to give you a path to run on, here's an idea.

For each line of the input, before you pass it into the `csv.reader`, run this code on it:

```python
def pick_my_edge_case(line):
    line = line.rstrip('\r\n')  # Remove newlines from the end of the line
    last_field = line.rsplit(',', 1)  # Look at the stuff after the last comma
    after_last_quote = last_field.rsplit('"', 1)  # See if there's more after the last quote
    if after_last_quote and last_field.count('"') % 2 == 0:
        # There are an even number of quotes, so they are removed, but we don't want them to be.
        # Add a new quote with a special marker that we can look for to remove later.
        line += '"$$$REMOVETHISQUOTE$$$'
    return line + '\r\n'  # Make sure we've got the newline in the final output

def undo_my_pick(processed_line):
    if processed_line:
        # Remove the special sequence from the line
        processed_line[-1] = processed_line[-1].replace('"$$$REMOVETHISQUOTE$$$', '')
    return processed_line
```

Like I said, I think it's quite possible that your case is more nuanced than your comment demonstrates, but hopefully this can give you an idea of what you might be able to do.

I have solved this with the following subclass of `CsvRenderer`. It depends on `more_itertools` library.

    # python
    from collections import OrderedDict
    from six import text_type


    # contrib
    from more_itertools import unique_everseen
    from rest_framework_csv.renderers import CSVRenderer


    class OrderedCsvRenderer(
        CSVRenderer,
    ):

        def tablize(self, data, header=None, labels=None):
            """
            Convert a list of data into a table.
            """
            if data:

                # First, flatten the data (i.e., convert it to a list of
                # dictionaries that are each exactly one level deep).  The key for
                # each item designates the name of the column that the item will
                # fall into.
                data = self.flatten_data(data)
                data.header = header or data.header

                # Get the set of all unique headers, and sort them (unless already provided).
                if not data.header:
                    headers = []
                    for item in data:
                        headers.extend(item.keys())

                    unique_fields = list(
                        unique_everseen(headers)
                    )

                    ordered_fields = OrderedDict()
                    for item in unique_fields:
                        field = item.split(".")
                        field = field[0]
                        if field in ordered_fields:
                            ordered_fields[field].append(item)
                        else:
                            ordered_fields[field] = [item]

                    data.header = []
                    for fields in ordered_fields.itervalues():
                        for field in fields:
                            data.header.append(field)

                # Create a row for each dictionary, filling in columns for which the
                # item has no data with None values.
                rows = []
                for item in data:
                    row = []
                    for key in data.header:
                        row.append(item.get(key, None))
                    rows.append(row)

                # Return your "table", with the headers as the first row.
                if labels:
                    return [[labels.get(x, x) for x in data.header]] + rows
                else:
                    return [data.header] + rows

            else:
                return []

        def nest_flat_item(self, flat_item, prefix):
            """
            Given a "flat item" (a dictionary exactly one level deep), nest all of
            the column headers in a namespace designated by prefix.  For example:

             header... | with prefix... | becomes...
            -----------|----------------|----------------
             'lat'     | 'location'     | 'location.lat'
             ''        | '0'            | '0'
             'votes.1' | 'user'         | 'user.votes.1'

            """
            nested_item = OrderedDict()
            for header, val in flat_item.items():
                nested_header = self.level_sep.join([prefix, header]) if header else prefix
                nested_item[nested_header] = val
            return nested_item

        def flatten_list(self, l):
            flat_list = OrderedDict()
            for index, item in enumerate(l):
                index = text_type(index)
                flat_item = self.flatten_item(item)
                nested_item = self.nest_flat_item(flat_item, index)
                flat_list.update(nested_item)
            return flat_list

        def flatten_dict(self, d):
            flat_dict = OrderedDict()
            for key, item in d.items():
                key = text_type(key)
                flat_item = self.flatten_item(item)
                nested_item = self.nest_flat_item(flat_item, key)
                flat_dict.update(nested_item)
            return flat_dict

The current str key support in dist-kvstore is very limited:
- it assumes all workers have the same key set
- it assumes the keys are initialized in the same order
To truly support str-key with dist-kvstore, we also need to record a int key->str key mapping on the server side, which is not implemented for now. One way to implement this is just use rank0 server as a coordinator, which serializes all the key requests when keys are initialized. The workers have to send an extra message to the coordinator to get the int key during str key initialization. You're welcome to try to fix it and post a PR for this.

Found something interesting while troubleshooting. I can reproduce the hang with the following code in a test script, which is akin to how GitPython invokes Popen():

```python
proc = Popen('git fetch -v'.split(),
             stdin=None, stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE,
             close_fds=True)
(stdout_value, stderr_value) = proc.communicate()
```
If I then change `stderr=PIPE` to `stderr=None`, the hang goes away and the script runs fine. It would appear that python's subprocess.Popen() function may not be handling stderr in a way that plays well with the credentials helper.

I did some more searching and found a bug report in the vcstools project that looks identical to what I'm seeing here:

https://github.com/vcstools/vcstools/issues/67

It looks like what they do is disable the stdout/stderr PIPE from the command to work around the issue. That might be too harsh. Another thought might be, as a configurable option, to selectively disable the stderr pipe for the git fetch command (and just rely on the exit status to tell success/failure). What do you think? I realize that GitPython is in no way responsible for the behavior of subprocess.Popen() -- just trying to see if there might be a way to enable this use case without introducing unwanted complications for GitPython.

Let’s just track it here.

PyPI performs this normalisation to ensure package names are of a certain format, as specified in [PEP 503](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0503/). `querystringsafe_base64` is not a valid package name, and is normalised by PyPI to `querystringsafe-base64`, which is the “real” package name PyPI (and Python packaging tools) uses to recognise the package. If you look into the `site-packages` directory you installed the package in, you would find a `querystringsafe-base64-<version>.egg-info` which reflects this.

Since the lock file is generated from the actual installation, it contains the normalised, actual identifier of the package on PyPI. It is unnecessary to convert the name back to what you entered because there is not inherent advantage (aside from user preference), and would require an extra conversion when you use the lockfile later. The lockfile is not intended to be manually inspected by the user anyway.

Also note that if you use the auto-append feature (`pipenv install <package>`) instead of manually adding the package name into Pipfile, all your package names in Pipfile would also be converted to the normalised form as the lock file.

the flow for installing with "install -r requirements.txt" and "install tox" are quite different. Attached file `pipenv_r_output.txt` and `pipenv_output.txt` are the verbose output. They both output the same `Pipfile.lock` file.

Both `Pipfile.lock` files are incorrectly marked tox 2.9.1. For install with "-r", it would install packages according to the `Pipfile.lock`, and hence, tox version 2.9.1 is installed.

For both methods, subsequent command `pipenv sync` would update the tox to 2.9.1, which is quite inconsistent.

Your solution does provide a remedy for problems such as mine, and I appreciate that.

I think, though, that setuptools could be kinder to its users. I don't see much point in sending users off to google what this problem with UnicodeDecodeError and what's the accepted workaround ("oh, so I have to set this environment variable and then it works? thanks mate. But why can't things just work? Sigh..."). I mean, what's the benefit from that virtual legwork? setuptools can just catch the error, consult a hard-coded list of "bad egg-infos", see that the egg-info in question is a known troublemaker, and silently disregard the error. 

I see why you'd be concerned about incentivizing maintainers of other packages to fix the encoding of the egg-info. For that, I think it sufficient to require that elements are not added to the "bad eggs" list until a ticket is opened against the offending package in the appropriate place. If the package is actively maintained, then the owner is very likely to care that his package is listed in a "hall of shame" such as this, so I think this is incentive enough to fix the root cause.

Consider that my workstation's OS has reached end-of-life. Even if rpl were fixed in sourceforge, it would not help me much (I neither have root access to my machine nor do I know how to create and install a custom ubuntu package to replace the egg-info with a good one). There are lots of users who cannot fix the root cause, and I don't see much point in disrupting their work. Hence my suggestion of a "bad eggs" list.

Regarding Java and Go: they're very prescriptive about how developers lay
out their source directories. People put up with that because they're
solely used as application development languages, and not for ad hoc
scripting.

Regarding Perl: if I recall correctly, the package membership declaration
has to appear in the module source file as well as being part of the
directory layout

I think for setuptools, it would be fine to have a "source only" marker
file that was used to lay down a directory on target systems. I'd suggest
"__nsdir__" as the name, though - you're requesting that the build system
add that directory to the namespace package, not defining the namespace
package directly.

This seems more like a problem in the design of how [routes are keyed](https://github.com/twisted/klein/blob/master/src/klein/app.py#L202) in Klein:

```python
kwargs.setdefault('endpoint', f.__name__)
```

This makes the name of the function or method subtly important, while IMHO the name of the function shouldn't matter (and shouldn't break things).

I _think_ this could be solved simply by changing that line to something like:

```python
kwargs.setdefault('endpoint', '.'.join([f.__module__, f.__name__])
```

or perhaps even:

```python
kwargs.setdefault('endpoint', "{}_{}".format(f.__name__, id(f))
```

This would give every function a unique endpoint identifier, and (please correct me if I am wrong) fit more closely to the spirit of what one would expect to happen.

Sorry, I didn't get your point. If you do `save({"_id": ObjectId(...), ...})` it should:

1. Replace existing document if there is document with same `_id` in collection
2. Add new document to collection, if collection doesn't have one with same `_id`

`update` with `upsert=True` does exactly this. With `upsert=False` it could do only the # 1.

I agree that this can be controlled by `min_queued_messages` but this in non obvious to the end user. I think there is a strong case based on ease of use for the client to keep this from happening. As a user, when i have base messages that are under the default 1mb, i should never hit a message too large error without having to understand the internals of kafka's compressed message protocol. 

Thinking of copying the java param `max_request_size`. The only remaining question is should this setting apply to all produce requests or just compressed requests? Hi Philippe,

You shouldn't need two clusters for production, but you might consider using two domain names that ultimately route to the same cluster, e.g.:

* `api.example.com` -> point to Django app.
* `realtime.example.com` -> point to Fanout Cloud -> set `api.example.com` as the origin with Rewrite Host Header disabled.

Then client requests to either domain will arrive at the same Django app. Be sure your `settings.ALLOWED_HOSTS` is configured to allow both domains.

For SSL you'll need to manually update the certs in the Fanout control panel. We are considering adding support for LetsEncrypt in Fanout Cloud later this year.

```
    app = QtWidgets.QApplication(sys.argv)

    if hasattr(QtCore.Qt, 'AA_UseHighDpiPixmaps'):
        app.setAttribute(QtCore.Qt.AA_UseHighDpiPixmaps)

Easy enough to reproduce:

```
my_fields = ['key']
issues = jira.search_issues(jql, fields=my_fields)
# `my_fields` has been modified
assert(my_fields == ['key'])
```

The reason for this is pretty simple: `JIRA.search_issues` modifies the `fields` list you pass to it directly, using the field names from `/rest/api/2/field` to correct any fields where the "friendly" name is being used (e.g. "Key" as the name for "issuekey"). Lists are mutable in python, and you're simply passing a reference to it, so when `JIRA.search_issues` makes any modifications to your list, those modifications are being made to the original list.

To fix this, I would suggest modifying `JIRA.search_issues` to make a copy of the `fields` list. There's already some code at the beginning of `JIRA.search_issues` to handle the case where `fields=None`, so it should be simple enough to modify this:

```
        if fields is None:
            fields = []

        if isinstance(fields, string_types):
            fields = fields.split(",")
```

To this:

```
        if fields is None:
            fields = []
        
        if isinstance(fields, list):
            fields = fields.copy()

        if isinstance(fields, string_types):
            fields = fields.split(",")
```

That's a server-side decision. 

If the server prompts the client for authentication for every request, then yes, every request will need to be authenticated.

The application developer can chose to provide a secure/signed session cookie, and authenticate clients with that if it's available, and fall-back on kerberos if it is not, but we can't make that happen from within this library.

I believe this patch does the right thing with both requests 1.1 and newer versions:

```
--- a/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py
+++ b/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py
@@ -149,6 +149,7 @@ def authenticate_user(self, response, **kwargs):
         response.content
         response.raw.release_conn()
 
+        self.deregister(response)
         _r = response.connection.send(response.request, **kwargs)
         _r.history.append(response)
 
```

but it breaks a bunch of tests and I can't see any nice way to fix them, since the tests are poking around in the internals of HTTPKerberosAuth with mocks, instead of actually following the normal requests flow.

        """
        Builds a list of values provided by :py:attr:`.sources` looked up on the target ``obj``.
        Each source may provide a value as a 2-tuple of ``(default_value, rich_value)``, where
        ``default_value`` is the sortable raw value, and ``rich_value`` is possibly something else
        that can be coerced to a string for display purposes. The ``rich_value`` could also be a
        string with HTML in it.

        If no 2-tuple is given, then ``default_value`` and ``rich_value`` are taken to be the same.

        Columns with multiple :py:attr:`.sources` will have their ``rich_value`` coerced to a string
        and joined with :py:attr:`.separator`, and this new concatenated string becomes the final
        ``rich_value`` for the whole column.

        If all :py:attr:`.sources` are ``None``, :py:attr:`.empty_value` will be used as the
        ``rich_value``.
        """
```

One potential use case for this feature: when you are using Lambda versioning and want to have a beta deploy and prod deploy in the same CodePipeline updating the same Lambda function. The CodePipeline flow goes something like this:

1. Source: git master merge triggers pipeline
2. CodeBuild: builds Lambda package zip, runs `aws cloudformation package`, and hands off output template and `beta`/`prod` config files
3. CloudFormation: creates and executes `beta` changeset, which replaces the `$LATEST` for the named function, publishes a version, and updates the alias `beta` to point to that version
4. Manual Approval
5. CloudFormation: created and executes `prod` changeset, which updates the alias `prod` to point to the version being pointed to by `beta`

We could accomplish this by having two totally separate Lambda functions published in different stacks, but then we're not technically cutting over to the deployed function version that has been tested and approved.

If the Task could do the math right and combine the progress % for multi-part transfers, that would be great.

One hacky thing we could though, is fake that math on the client-side. So, if importing two channels from a disk, we do this:

* 0% for beginning of channel 1
* 50 * (Percentage for channel 1)
* 50 + (Percentage for channel 2) * 50

And for n channels,

* 0% for beginning of channel 1
* (100 / n) * (Percentage for channel 1)
* m / n * (100 / n ) * (Percentage for channel m)

This is sort of possible, because we can tell when a new channel is switched-over too (the task id changes), which lets us increment the value of m.

What is the value of even showing point accomplishments for guests? If it's to incentivize creating an account, then this notification should be somewhere else as outside of content popups. Putting them in content popups is an annoyance, furthers unnecessary clutter, especially if the user definitely doesn't want to sign up or if the context uses guest accounts. Also, the alert referenced in the original post looks like an error, which we don't want to convey after every content mastery. 

Another concern is the misleading text. The guest isn't technically saving any points at all, so in the short term, the text should be revised to "Create an account to start saving points". To condense, we can omit "sign in" from the verbiage because "Create an account" alludes to obtaining an account, and the user will remember to sign in to their account, if they have one. It sucks to set up the expectation that points from content they've engaged with so far will be saved. 

For a future fix I think it makes sense to **remove point accomplishments for guests** and place a "create account to save points" notification somewhere high level in the interface, so that
1) the guest can understand at all times what they're missing out on when engaging as a guest + the benefits of an account
2) the guest avoids being bombarded by this sign-up/create account spam after ever content mastery.

For those wondering how we ended up here, in September 2013 when we moved to asymptotically approaching 1.0 I expected a release of 1.0 by the end of 2013. Alas, that didn't happen, for a variety of reasons (primarily the spec having some large changes and not wanting to ship something out of date, and lack of time for me and lack of other contributors). It's worthwhile pointing out that html5lib in its origins was essentially a research project and as a support tool to those suggesting changes to the spec, as such it had some weird features and some weird oddities in it.

Now, perhaps 0.99999999 should have been 0.100000000 to clarify the changes, but that's also a confusing version number.

Also in hindsight, we probably should've shipped one of the releases in 2013 as 1.0.

Ultimately, what's blocking shipping 1.0 now is having something up-to-date with the spec and having documentation.

Well, the issue here I think is that we have started streaming the pull logs (i.e. started writing the HTTP response body) which means we can't modify the HTTP status code. SInce no error was found at the moment the request is processed, the status code is 200.

An error during the pull is then simply reported in the logs themselves and since we have a very hands off approach to the data that comes out of there, docker-py doesn't transform it into an exception. The tradeoff is speed of execution (no JSON parsing / extra processing) VS convenience (Having an exception raised when an error occurs in the pull/push/build logs).

For this specifically:
```json
{"errorDetail":{"message":"HTTP code: 403"},"error":"HTTP code: 403"}
```

Not at all.  And now that you've said it, I realize that py35 support is only required for py-evm.  Trinity can be py36 only and I think I'm good with that.  (ping @carver @gsalgado )

Reason that py-evm has to support py35 is that Web3.py supports python3.5 and it's `EthTester` provider is based on the `eth-tester` library, which has a `py-evm` backend, and thus, py-evm requires support for py35.

Also, we have sort of been taking our cues **loosely** from Django in terms of dropping support for older versions of python.

Implementation of this PR would require within the `# prepend, append, add` section of `cli/main_config.py` that, if the key is `channels`, do something like

```python
channel = Channel(item)
session = CondaSession()
response = session.head(join_url(channel.base_url, 'noarch', 'repodata.json'))
response.raise_for_status()
```

we would need to make sure

1. proxy servers, including proxy servers requiring auth, are properly handled
2. remote servers requiring various types of auth that conda general supports (e.g. HTTP Basic Auth) are properly supported
3. If `context.offline == True` and `channel.scheme != 'file'`, we also skip validation.

I think we need more community discussion before we add an http request to `--set`, `--add`, `--append`, or `--prepend` commands on `conda config`.

In the meantime, I think there are two more immediate steps we can take.

First, we can add the above logic to `conda config --validate`.  For now though, I'd want to confine any addition here to `cli/main_config.py`.  We can refactor to different parts of the code later.

In this case, the logic would be something like

```python
session = CondaSession()
for chn in context.channels:
    channel = Channel(chn)
    response = session.head(join_url(channel.base_url, 'noarch', 'repodata.json'))
    response.raise_for_status()
```

Second, we can discuss changing the default value of `context.allow_non_channel_urls` from `True` to `False`.  The area of code affected by this config is [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.15/conda/core/repodata.py#L164-L242).  It is related to #3739 and #4238.

> It should be used when getting indices in IndexList 

The chunking utility is for preventing a URL-length overrun when making requests.  Elasticsearch can't handle it when a URL longer than 4K bytes is sent.  How exactly would I limit this on the _request_ end?

As it stands, the flow is as follows:

1. At the end of Class instance initialization, it calls [`self.__get_indices()`](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L31).
1. Instance method `__get_indices` calls utility function [`get_indices`](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L66)
1. Utility function `get_indices` calls the [`get_settings` API](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/utils.py#L566-L569) and extracts a list of indices.
1. [If there are indices](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L68), it will [loop through all discovered indices](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L69), and [create an empty dictionary schema for index metadata](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L74-L90).  No API calls are made in this step.
1. It populates the metadata [by calling a function](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L71), which actually uses the [chunking utility function](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.2.0/curator/indexlist.py#L148).

So far as using chunking in the IndexList class is concerned, it sounds more appropriate to use Elasticsearch's wildcarding and/or regular expressions to pull in a limited number of matching indices _first._  This is a feature I've long contemplated adding but have not yet gotten around to implementing.

> **additionally** use a configurable number of indices as chunk size too

This cannot be done for the reasons stipulated already: Chunking is about URL line length, not a count of index names.  It could be possible to define the chunk size, but that is less effective, I think than changing the index count to start with, as already suggested.  I may still add it.

Actually, the error message results from a problem with the order of operations.  This is revealed here:

```
from voluptuous import *

schemas = [
    Schema({Optional('exclude'): All(Any(int, bool), Coerce(bool))}),
    Schema({Optional('exclude'): All(Any(bool, int), Coerce(bool))}),
]

test = [
    {'exclude':0},
    {'exclude':1},
    {'exclude':'0'},
    {'exclude':'1'},
    {'exclude':'false'},
    {'exclude':'true'},
    {'exclude':'False'},
    {'exclude':'True'},
    {'exclude':False},
    {'exclude':True},
]

for s in schemas:
    print('{0}'.format(s))
    for t in test:
        try:
            result = s(t)
            print('   PASS: Schema({0}) = {1}'.format(t, s(t)))
        except Exception as e:
            print('   FAIL: Schema({0}). Exception: {1}'.format(t, e))
    print
```

Here are the results:

```
<Schema({'exclude': All(Any([<type 'int'>, <type 'bool'>]), Coerce(bool, msg=None), msg=None)}, extra=PREVENT_EXTRA, required=False) object at 0x10ef56e90>
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': 0}) = {'exclude': False}
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': 1}) = {'exclude': True}
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': '0'}). Exception: expected int for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': '1'}). Exception: expected int for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'false'}). Exception: expected int for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'true'}). Exception: expected int for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'False'}). Exception: expected int for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'True'}). Exception: expected int for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': False}) = {'exclude': False}
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': True}) = {'exclude': True}

<Schema({'exclude': All(Any([<type 'bool'>, <type 'int'>]), Coerce(bool, msg=None), msg=None)}, extra=PREVENT_EXTRA, required=False) object at 0x10ef5f310>
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': 0}) = {'exclude': False}
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': 1}) = {'exclude': True}
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': '0'}). Exception: expected bool for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': '1'}). Exception: expected bool for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'false'}). Exception: expected bool for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'true'}). Exception: expected bool for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'False'}). Exception: expected bool for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   FAIL: Schema({'exclude': 'True'}). Exception: expected bool for dictionary value @ data['exclude']
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': False}) = {'exclude': False}
   PASS: Schema({'exclude': True}) = {'exclude': True}
```

As you can see, strings are not coerced into booleans, but integers are.  With the proper order, `Any(bool, int)`, it gives the proper message, `expected bool`.  That's what actually needs to change here, as it otherwise does what it is supposed to do.  It's supposed to cast a boolean, and `0` and `1` can be boolean, as can `False` and `True`, but `"0"` and `"1"`, and `"true"`, and `"false"` cannot, capitalized or not.

When login to AAD, your account can be associated with multiple tenants. AAD makes it possible to deal with this by exposing a particular endpoint, where the tenant parameter is not known up front. `common` is  such a particular value which tells Azure AD that the requester is not mandating any particular tenant, so any tenant will do. If you are using org-id, the particular tenant you land will be your org's default tenant, say, `microsoft.com`.  Under this context, Azure CLI will hit the common tenant first and with the access token, we can use ARM service's `list_tenants' and `list_subscriptions` to further discover all subscriptions you have accesses to.

But Azure Stack's AAD endpoint doesn't support `common`; hence we need to change. 

For regular login, you can leverage this new arg if you like to skip tenants you are not interested,

Ok, it is ELB actually.

`Keep-Alive` header is non-standard, the standard should use `Connection: close` and assume keep-alive by default.

After keepalive timeout expiring server has nothing to do but close the socket.
The server cannot send HTTP response immediately on expiring: HTTP protocol requests HTTP request from client first.
Server cannot postpone connection closing too: client may never send a request, the server leaks opened socket in this case.
What can you do is increasing keepalive timeout in aiohttp.

I pretty sure every HTTP server (NGINX, Apache, HAProxy) has the same behavior.

I definitely think it would be nice to be able to have the notebooks automatically update the metadata, but there are a few reasons I'm hesitant to add this:

1. There is not a good way to do this from the notebook interface without reimplementing all the schema handling functionality in javascript. The reason the native nbformat stuff works well is because the notebook version is updated before the notebook is sent to the browser, but in the case of nbgrader, there is no way to do this until after the notebook reaches the browser. I could create a server route that updates the notebook on disk, but this would then require the user to refresh the notebook page to get the update. If they do not refresh, this could potentially cause further confusion with getting errors like "the notebook has been updated on disk, do you want to overwrite it or refresh?"
2. I am worried about automatically updating metadata in the rare case that it somehow breaks someones assignment. That's why all the error messages tell the user to back up their notebooks first before updating the metadata.

Yes and no. The `jacobian` function gives you the complete `jacobian` of your vector function `f`. with respect to the argument `x` or `y`. See: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacobian_matrix_and_determinant). What the `elementwis_grad` function does is take the partial derivative of `output[i]` with respect to `input[i]`. These partial derivates are equivalent to the diagonal elements of the Jacobian matrix. In your function `abs(x - y)` all the off-diagonal elements are zero. However, this does not mean that the Jacobian matrix and `elementwise_grad` give the same output.

Again, I can probably best illustrate this with an example, by adding an interaction between `x` and `y` to the function:
```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import jacobian

def f(x, y):
    return np.abs(x - y + np.dot(x,y))

jacobian_f_wrt_x = jacobian(f, 0) # 0 indicates first input element in f(x, y)
jacobian_f_wrt_y = jacobian(f, 1) # 1 indicates second input element in f(x, y)

x = np.arange(-4, 6, 2, dtype=float)
y = np.zeros(5) + 1.5

print(jacobian_f_wrt_x(x, y))
print(jacobian_f_wrt_y(x, y))
```

Now the output has changed to
```
[[-2.5 -1.5 -1.5 -1.5 -1.5]
 [-1.5 -2.5 -1.5 -1.5 -1.5]
 [-1.5 -1.5 -2.5 -1.5 -1.5]
 [ 1.5  1.5  1.5  2.5  1.5]
 [ 1.5  1.5  1.5  1.5  2.5]]
[[ 5.  2.  0. -2. -4.]
 [ 4.  3.  0. -2. -4.]
 [ 4.  2.  1. -2. -4.]
 [-4. -2.  0.  1.  4.]
 [-4. -2.  0.  2.  3.]]
```

I'd like to nudge everyone in the direction of simplicity.

Here are a two options I can think of that have synthesized your feedback (one is actually much simpler than the other):

1. Names expire after a minimum of 1 year. Multiple years can be paid for up front. After the expiration period names may enter a lockdown period of 2 months, during which they may still be renewed. After that, they either (a) expire and can be picked up again or (b) are tombstoned and can be renewed and reactivated.
2. Names don't expire. They just cost 2x or 3x what they would cost for 1 year (squatting disincentive) and then they can be kept forever. Simple logic. No weird rules.

>a hookimpl should be able to declare the default for backward compatibility (say a hook adds a new parameter you want to use if availiable, but still support older versions of the program that did not support/supply it

@RonnyPfannschmidt Can't we accomplish this **and** what @hpk42 thinks the precedence order should be by simply **not strictly requiring** `@hookimpl`s to declare all `kwargs` declared in the `hookspec`? 

In other words, in `_MultiCall.execute()` we only call the `@hookimpl` with keyword arguments declared on the `@hookimpl` that we were able to discover through pre-inspection (which we're already now doing [in varnames](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy.py#L661) but we just need to [return `defaults` and assign to a new `HookImpl.defaults`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy.py#L786)).

From the `@hookspec`s perspective this means `args` are **strictly** required and `kwargs` are **opt in**. This allows for a couple interesting possibilities, namely:
- a `@hookimpl` can choose **not to** declare a `kwarg` it doesn't need (or hasn't yet implemented)
-  if a `@hookimpl` defines a `kwarg` that is not provided by the caller, everything continues as normal and the default is used
- a hook call (`_HookCaller.__call__()`) can pass through **new `kwargs`** not defined in the spec which can be handled in the `@hookimpl`s using `**kwargs`

As an example of a `@hookimpl` which utilizes all of these concepts:
```python
 @hookimpl
 def myhookimpl(required_arg1, required_arg2, new_maybe_provided=None, **kwargs):
     # must process the required args
     do_something(arg1)
     do_something(arg2)
     
     if new_maybe_provided is not None:
         # new enough version that this kwarg is provided and defined in the spec
         do_something(new_maybe_provided)
      
      # call to hook may have passed in additional data not yet defined in the spec
      if 'my_new_kwarg' in kwargs:
          do_something(kwargs['my_new_kwarg'])
   ...
 ```
But, an older hook which declares **no `kwargs`** would also work since `kwargs` are **opt in**. So when `_HookCaller.__call__()` and later `_MultiCall.execute()` are invoked they only pass in kwargs if they are *detected* (through pre-inspection) as being declared on the below function.

```python
 @hookimpl
 def myhookimpl(required_arg1, required_arg2):
     # must process the required args
     do_something(arg1)
     do_something(arg2)

# this still works since kwargs are opt in
myhookimpl(required_arg1='blah', required_arg2='doggy', new_maybe_provided=10, my_new_kwarg='yey')

```
The spec would look like:
```python
@hookspec
def myhookimpl(required_arg1, required_arg2, new_maybe_provided=None):
    '''A hook that requires args 1 and 2 and a new_maybe_provided kwarg value
    '''
```
This ^ allows us to transition new arguments into hooks by first including them as optional `kwargs` (where we can then optionally emit warnings if old hooks aren't implementing them) and later transition them to required `args` after some known version.

There's kind of an interesting question about how to handle certs that were issued more recently than the cert that the user wants to use. I think we had an idea about renaming them so that they no longer appear to be in the same lineage, maybe?

The problem with this is that if the user reverts a symlink to an older cert version in the same lineage, but has autodeploy turned on, the renewer might end up changing the symlink back again in the other direction (reasoning that there's a newer certificate available within the same lineage, so once the autodeploy period is reached, that newer certificate ought to be used).

I was able to generate and install the LE cert for GAE via the manual plugin. 
OSX Mavericks

1. Generate private key and csr
	```
	$ openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout example.com.private.key -nodes -sha512 -subj "/CN=example.com" -reqexts SAN -out example.com.csr.der -outform der -config <(cat /System/Library/OpenSSL/openssl.cnf <(printf "[SAN]\nsubjectAltName=DNS:example.com,DNS:www.example.com"))
	```

2. Two files should be generated: ``example.com.private.key`` and ``example.com.csr.der``

3. Run ./letencrypt-auto
	```
	./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a manual --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview --csr example.com.csr.der
	```

4. Depending on your app, you will need to make changes to generate the verification text response as prompted. If you're generating the cert for both www. and the naked domain, you will be challenged twice with 2 different texts.

5. When the certificate and chain files are successfully generated, go to https://console.developers.google.com/project/_/appengine/settings/certificates and choose "Upload a new certificate".

6. For "Unencrypted PEM encoded RSA private key", upload ``example.com.private.key``
   For "PEM encoded X.509 public key certificate", upload ``0001_chain.pem``

7. Proceed as the app engine console instructs. If all goes well, you will be given the choice to enable the uploaded SSL cert for your domain.

Wow, this is bizarre. I cut-and-pasted the `fromutc()` function from the Python 3.4.3 source distribution into `tzfile()` to see where it was going wrong (this is, mind you, not a function that has changed whatsoever between Python 3.3 and 3.5), and it fixed the issue.

I'm thinking this may be something extremely specific to the Ubuntu release somehow. I don't think it's something I can (or rather, should) fix on the dateutil end, unless I'm missing something.

For now, here's a workaround that patches the correct `fromutc` function into `tzfile`:

```python
import datetime
from dateutil import tz

class tzinfo_patch(object):
    def fromutc(self, dt):
        "datetime in UTC -> datetime in local time."

        if not isinstance(dt, datetime.datetime):
            raise TypeError("fromutc() requires a datetime argument")
        if dt.tzinfo is not self:
            raise ValueError("dt.tzinfo is not self")

        dtoff = dt.utcoffset()
        if dtoff is None:
            raise ValueError("fromutc() requires a non-None utcoffset() "
                             "result")

        # See the long comment block at the end of this file for an
        # explanation of this algorithm.
        dtdst = dt.dst()
        if dtdst is None:
            raise ValueError("fromutc() requires a non-None dst() result")
        delta = dtoff - dtdst
        if delta:
            dt += delta
            dtdst = dt.dst()
            if dtdst is None:
                raise ValueError("fromutc(): dt.dst gave inconsistent "
                                 "results; cannot convert")
        return dt + dtdst

class patched_tzfile(tzinfo_patch, tz.tzfile):
    pass

def _patched_gettz(*args, **kwargs):
    tzi = tz.gettz(*args, **kwargs)

    if isinstance(tzi, tz.tzfile):
        return patched_tzfile(tzi._filename)

gettz = _patched_gettz

LONDON = gettz('Europe/London')
UTC = tz.tzutc()

dt1 = datetime.datetime(2016, 4, 5, 12, 35, tzinfo=UTC)
dt_l = dt1.astimezone(LONDON)

print(dt1)          # 2016-04-05 12:35:00+00:00
print(dt_l)         # 2016-04-05 13:35:00+01:00
```

It does rely on a private member function, and I haven't tested it thoroughly, so it might be fragile, but hopefully it will help in the short term if you are stuck on a broken version of Python.

I will try and track down the python source code that Ubuntu used for their 3.4.3 build to see if they changed something and if there's anything I can / should do about it.

The reason that `pytz` works, by the way, is that they use their own implementation of `fromutc`. We may switch to our own implementation in dateutil-2.6.0 if necessary for handling edge cases, in which case this issue will be fixed without upgrading. However, I don't want to duplicate the code from Python just because one particular build had an issue with it.

By the way, exdate and rdate are allowed to be dates without time, so solution 2. above would be incorrect:
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5545#section-3.8.5.1
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5545#section-3.8.5.2

I think the cleanest approach would be to have two versions of the rrule machinery: one which works only with datetime, and one which works only with dates.  That is, DTSTART, EXDATE and RDATE must all have the same type (either all datetimes, or all dates).  I have no idea if this is conforming with RFC5545 though, since it is not very clear on this point.

This way of handling related links works, but changes the way the views must be organized. 

In my project we have a model `Departments` that has a to-many relationship to `Person`. The related link, for a department with pk 2, would be `/departments/2/people`, and the self link (for the relationship view) is `/departments/2/relationships/people`. The related link routes to the same `PersonViewSet` we use for the `/people` endpoint, where we have overwritten the `get_queryset` method to filter by department if the `department_pk` kwarg is passed to the view.

We find that this is convenient because if there is any view(set)-level logic that is specific to the person model, we only need to put it in one place. An example would be customizing the way the serializer gets instantiated.

Your way of handling the related link suggests an arrangement that is sort of the inverse of this -- to have one view(set) that handles all related links for a given model. You would probably need to write code (for example in `get_serializer` if it's a viewset) to pick the right serializer based on the name of the related field.

The reason we wrote RelationshipView under the assumption that there's one RelationshipView for a given object is mainly because the relationship endpoint only needs to serialize type names and IDs for the related objects, not the entire objects themselves; and it makes sense for the RelationshipView to "know" about the parent object, since it will be changing that object (for a to-one relationship) or its many-to-many relation(s) (for to-many relationships). The related link view, on the other hand, is more like a filter.

I think your way of doing it is okay, but it will break projects that are already set up the other way.

``` python
import pymongo

But usually I'd also get the run-entry from the database, because for that you don't need the experiment or the observer:

``` python
import pymongo
import gridfs

client = pymongo.MongoClient()  # assuming a local MongoDB
fs = gridfs.GridFS(client.sacred)  # assuming database name is 'sacred'

runs = client.sacred.runs
# Now get run 12 from the database
run_entry = runs.find_one({'_id': 12})  # BTW: you can query by whatever you want here.

with open('file_to_save_your_artifact.pkl', 'wb') as f:
    f.write(fs.get(run_entry['artifacts'][0]).read())
```

        @ex.automain

  * the observers coordinate their ``_id`` such that they match between databases/filestorage/...

Any interaction between floating point numbers and real numbers will automatically be non-linear, because the floating-point operations are highly non-linear, even discontinuous from the real-theory's point of view. The encoding shown here does not call real-multiplication directly, but fp.mul is just as non-linear a constraint, and in some ways it's even worse for the real-theory, because it doesn't see any constraints, but it still has to magically guess the right numbers (sometimes it gets lucky, but most of the times it won't). On this particular problem it doesn't get lucky even when we fix `res` to 25.0, as suggested. But, if we fix `res` to 0.0 then it does get lucky. So yes, the warning goes both ways. 

There are no fixed-point numbers and there are no plans to introduce them because they can trivially be constructed from floating-point numbers by fixing the exponent to some desired constant value, so there's really no need for a separate class of functions. Also, the lower limit for exponent bits is 3, so "almost fixed point numbers"  can be used without additional constraints. Further, simple bit-vectors can also be used trivially to represent fixed-point numbers by defining bv = x * 2^32 for a 32-bit fixed-point number.

As far as I can tell, the main point of discussion is whether the to_*** functions should live on NWBFile or not. I understand why the to_*** syntax helps makes things feel familiar and it sounds like there is agreement that we want to have those functions. However, I'm not quite sure why those functions would need to live on NWBFile compared to a dedicated io package. Numpy, e.g., also does not have those functions live on the actual array but users call numpy.save, numpy.from_file, etc. The pynwb.to_hdf seems to be essentially that approach, and I think it works well. Ultimately, I don't think it makes a huge difference from a user perspective whether to call NWBFile.to_hdf or pynwb.to_hdf. However, from a software design perspective the latter seems much cleaner and easier to maintain.

> The idea is that upon committing changes to a module one would add their name to a list of authors given as a comment at the top of the file. Feel free to let me know how you feel about adding this to the workflow of a standard Pull Request.

I'm not at all interested for several reasons:

* `git blame` and GitHub's blame view are designed for this \*
* Not everyone who has contributed is working on the project any more (it happens - they change jobs, choose something else, people die) - people tend to forget why they did things a certain way in little chunks of the code base, it's your job to figure it out and leave it better than you found it

`wait_event` is just a different way to receive an event. It allows your code to yield to event loop and let other greenlets to run. Until the event happens or it timeouts, then the code is resumed.

Every function you register is not called directly. Instead a greenlet is scheduled to run it. You will need to yield excplicitly to let other stuff run.

Mujoco-py*

I have finally solved this issue. The trick appears to be to preload the correct GL libraries before running python. The fix is described in this issue: https://github.com/deepmind/dm_control/issues/3. A bit further down I found the exact command that worked for me. No changes to the codebase (such as @lobachevzky's PR) are required. I have Ubuntu 16.04 and Nvidia driver version 384 (other drivers may work, but adjust version number below accordingly).

The secret sauce is prepending `LD_PRELOAD=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libGLEW.so:/usr/lib/nvidia-384/libGL.so` to the python command. I also found that it was difficult to clear the Cython cache which was making it difficult to debug the issue. I modified `setup.py` to clear the cache on my machine, but if you just want to get a solution, try these steps:

1. Clone master of mujoco_py
2. From the root of the cloned directory, run:
  ` pip install --no-cache-dir -e .`
  to install the package.
3. To see the disco Fetch robot in the visualizer, run:
`LD_PRELOAD=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libGLEW.so:/usr/lib/nvidia-384/libGL.so python examples/disco_fetch.py `

> My questions is that: here it import a .js file while there actually is a .ts file, histogramCore.js, locating in the path, so how the compiler figure work which file to refer automatically?

Heh, I take your point. That's a bit confusing.

The practical answer is, "don't worry about it": the build system abstracts over this for you, and everything will continue to Just Work if you follow the patterns that you see in existing files (like the histogram dashboard). But in case you're curious, here's what's going on, as far as I understand it.

You write `histogramCore.ts` in TypeScript. Then, the `ts_web_library` rule uses the TypeScript compiler to compile `histogramCore.ts` into `histogramCore.js`, which is an ES6 file. In particular, it contains both browser-supported ES6 features (classes, etc.) and ES6 module features (imports, exports). Other files will want to import _this_ compiled JavaScript file, so they use the `.js` extension instead of the `.ts` extension. Eventually, the Vulcanizer/closure compiler resolve all the imports and create the final bundle.

No, this is one of the dumb problems Windows has with interpretive languages like python. The thing is .py is encoded as a text file not an executable so Windows will by default always try to open a word processor or text editor. Which means to run the .py files you have to run through a shell or compile the text file to an executable there is no option to treat it like an executable by default and pass it through the interpreter unless you manually find python and set it by hand. There might be a way to do this last bit with a batch script but I'm not Windows savvy enough to know how. As I see it though most users on windows will just use the executables anyway, if not they're going to be using the scripts in a shell so in general it shouldn't be much of an issue.

Please merge #5901 instead of this PR. However, we should eventually also provide a way to use an existing non-Certbot-created key while also enrolling a cert for renewal (as even with the current version #5901, the only way to do that is with `--csr`, which doesn't enroll for renewal).

The workaround I am using is "post_hook" option for certbot. I think it is much more elegant.
In etc/letsencrypt/renewal/DOMAIN.conf, it looks as follows:
`post_hook = chmod 400 /usr/local/etc/letsencrypt/live/DOMAIN/privkey.pem; service apache24 reload; services dovecot reload; service sendmail reload`

This works (at least on FreeBSD) since `chmod` without any flags acts on the actual file to which the symlink is pointing. I am not sure if this is the same default for `chmod` on all systems. (You might need to adjust the path for your OS and you'd have your own "restart" commands)

> any idea why the errors didn't make their way out of cron?

`cron` normally sends mail if the command it runs produces any output which `certbot-auto renew --quiet` does if there are any failures. You can find information on how to configure `cron` to do this on websites like [this](https://serverfault.com/questions/54152/how-to-get-e-mail-from-failed-cron-jobs-in-ubuntu). If you want `cron` email sent to an external account, you'll need to configure a mail transfer agent (MTA) like `postfix`. You can find a guide on how to do this [here](https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-install-and-configure-postfix-as-a-send-only-smtp-server-on-ubuntu-14-04). Getting mail fron `cron` is a good practice as you can be notified of potential problems on your system beyond Certbot failures.

With that said, if you provided an email address to Certbot when you first started using it on this system and didn't unsubscribe from the Let's Encrypt expiration emails, Let's Encrypt should email you when your certificate approaches expiration and has not been renewed. These emails are only sent after Certbot should have already renewed your certificate, so if this is configured properly, you'll only get email if renewal is failing. If you answer cpu's question above, he should be able to help you figure out why you didn't get this message.

The polymorphic and Django API's work slightly different and a bit more high-level. by inheriting from `PolymorphicModel` you get all functionality in your model. It installs it's own `polymorphic_ctype` field that is used, so no `polymorphic_on` or `polymorphic_identity` fields are needed.

When you create a `ForeignKey` to a polymorphic model, it will give you polymorphic behavior by default. You can use `.non_polymorphic()` on the queryset to disable that. For example: `mymodel.related_field.non_polymorphic()`

ok, able to repro pretty easily. Just need to setup a test to check Pipfile before and after tox executes to make sure it's not adding the `deps` into it. 
There is a environment variable patch which touches a `Pipfile` in each venv directory. That *should* be used instead of the one in the root directory. 

1. On setup, clone the Pipfile to venv path, e.g. `.tox/pyxx/Pipfile` 
2. On execution, use PIPENV_PIPFILE as the venv pipfile, not the root one.

```bash
mkdir test-37
cd test-37
vi tox.ini # paste your example
pip3.6 install virtualenv
python3.6 -m virtualenv env/
source env/bin/activate
pip install pipenv tox tox-pipenv
tox -vv  # whilst watching Pipfile
```

It may not be easy to stream to multiple clients at the same time for every type of stream. Some container formats are not designed to be streamed in that way. MP4 requires some header data (`moov` box) to establish which tracks there are, etc. FLV is similar and has some metadata at the front of the file. MPEG(2)-TS is the only container that I can think of off the top of my head that might work, but you'd probably have issues with TS continuity.
