To ensure consistent code style, we would like to use [`yapf`](https://github.com/google/yapf), which is an unofficial Google project; Yet Another Python Formatter. We use a custom style that closely replicates our target styling for Python source code.

```
[style]
based_on_style = pep8
dedent_closing_brackets = true
split_complex_comprehension = true
split_before_logical_operator = true
split_before_expression_after_opening_paren = true
split_before_first_argument = true
split_before_named_assigns = true
each_dict_entry_on_separate_line = true
continuation_align_style = fixed
continuation_indent_width = 4
column_limit: 79
allow_multiline_dictionary_keys = false
allow_split_before_dict_value = false
```

This style is written into a local `.style.yapf` file in the repository. To install the development requirement, add `yapf` to the `requirements_dev.txt` file. To install in your working environment, type `pip install yapf`. To run, type `yapf -rd ...` where `...` is the working directory (`-rd` means "recursive, show diffs"). The preceding is the correct call to use in CI tests. The `yapf` tool also works great with a variety of editor environments.

You can locally disable the formatter by surrounding a block of code in `# yapf: disable` ... `# yapf: enable` comments. Please use this selectively, and be prepared to review/reassess in the future if the enclosed code changes.

To automatically style code whenever you commit, create a file called `.pre-commit-config.yaml` in your repo with the following:

```yaml
repos:
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-yapf
    sha: 'bb280692bd3b1e1f7fab174635ef9678f2452522'
    hooks:
    -   id: yapf
```

Install with:
```bash
pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install
```

Now you will automatically get styled code when you commit.

I'm not set up to test right now, but I did extensive testing on UART flow control back in May.  My notes record that the BLE400 does deassert RTS when the second byte is received and not read from the RXB register, and in this situation a Linux serial session with crtscts enabled does cause Linux to stop attempting to transmit.  I think that provides a "yes it does" answer to your question.

I have no reason to believe hardware flow control problems are involved in what I reported, though see below for what others might see.  The data support a hypothesis that the PC side isn't prepared to handle SLIP packets from multiple streams.

Do you have the potential patches to address this issue in branches on the github repositories?  If so, could you point me to them?  If I dig into this again I'd like to work with something more informative than a pre-packaged release.

(BTW: in my UART testing I confirmed [this behavior](https://devzone.nordicsemi.com/f/nordic-q-a/5200/does-the-uart-disable-rts-when-it-sees-cts-disabled) as well as numerous problems with ttyACMx communication on PCA10028 devices including loss of flow control signals when using certain USB hubs and writes above 64 bytes without MSD disabled on the OB_SAM3U.  So certainly UART firmware updates over ttyACMx with nRF5 development hardware on Linux are problematic.)

Searched DNs are cached when a user login. The relevant code is here:

https://github.com/django-auth-ldap/django-auth-ldap/blob/f07854293216b0a80629a56425d2dfc03b5e1b45/django_auth_ldap/backend.py#L522-L526

So, the old DN (`ou=SubDepartment1,ou=MainDepartment,dc=domain,dc=ru`) is cached and used for the next login attempt. As the DN is no longer correct, the bind fails.

I admit, this isn't well documented and can be confusing. The easiest workaround is to set `AUTH_LDAP_GROUP_CACHE_TIMEOUT` to 0. (Keep in mind, this will disable caching if you rely on it.)

I'm open to improvements in this area -- either docs or code -- if you'd like to make a proposal or open a PR.

I encountered this same error when I changed the canonical example to 

    from paver.easy import *
    from paver.setuputils import setup

    setup_params = dict(
        name="MyCoolProject",
        packages=['mycool'],
        version="1.0",
        url="http://www.blueskyonmars.com/",
        author="Kevin Dangoor",
        author_email="dangoor@gmail.com"
    )

    @task
    @needs(["distutils.command.sdist"])
    def sdist():
        """Generate docs and source distribution."""
        pass

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        setup(**setup_params)

When using distutils directly, I would call setup() in an '__main__' check in order to only invoke the distutils functionality when the setup.py script was executed. When using Paver, I now realize this is not proper usage and that setup() must be called before 'distutils.command.sdist' is invoked.

Perhaps Paver could provide a better error message in a situation like this.
